description: RTX Corporation is a tier-one aerospace and defense conglomerate operating through three divisions—Pratt & Whitney, Collins Aerospace, and Raytheon—supplying propulsion systems, avionics, and precision weapons to commercial airlines, militaries, and governments across more than 180 countries.

overview.title: Company at a Glance
paragraphs: RTX Corporation (NYSE: RTX), rebranded from Raytheon Technologies in 2023, emerged from the 2020 merger of United Technologies Corporation and Raytheon Company. Headquartered in Arlington, Virginia, RTX employs approximately 185,000 people and generates roughly $80 billion in annual revenue, positioning it as one of the two or three largest defense contractors in the world.

The company operates through three reporting segments: Pratt & Whitney (aircraft engines and propulsion), Collins Aerospace (avionics, interiors, and mission systems), and Raytheon (missiles, radar, electronic warfare, and command-and-control systems). This structure gives RTX an unusually broad footprint—selling to both the commercial aviation market and to the U.S. Department of Defense and allied governments simultaneously.

overview.title: Pratt & Whitney
paragraphs: Pratt & Whitney is RTX's commercial and military propulsion arm, producing some of the most widely flown jet engines in service. Its Geared Turbofan (GTF) engine family powers the Airbus A220 and A320neo series and has accumulated a large backlog, though the division was beset by a costly inspection and repair campaign beginning in 2023 after discovering powder-metal contamination in certain high-pressure turbine disks.

On the military side, Pratt & Whitney's F135 engine is the sole powerplant for the F-35 Lightning II, making it a program with generational lock-in—and all the pricing leverage that implies. The division also produces the F100 engine for F-15 and F-16 fighters and continues to compete for next-generation adaptive-cycle engine contracts.

overview.title: Collins Aerospace
paragraphs: Collins Aerospace is RTX's avionics, interiors, and mission systems segment, supplying flight controls, cockpit electronics, communication systems, satellite equipment, and passenger cabin products to virtually every major aircraft OEM. Its customer base spans commercial airlines, business aviation, and military operators, providing a degree of cyclical smoothing relative to pure-play defense peers.

The segment holds particularly strong positions in integrated modular avionics, environmental control systems, and space systems. Collins also supplies ISR (intelligence, surveillance, reconnaissance) sensors and electronic warfare systems that blur the line between its portfolio and Raytheon's, a feature RTX has leaned into as integrated multi-domain solutions become the prevailing procurement preference.

overview.title: Raytheon
paragraphs: The Raytheon segment produces missiles, air and missile defense systems, radar, and electronic warfare systems that have become central to Western deterrence strategy. High-profile programs include the Patriot PAC-3 air defense system, the AIM-120 AMRAAM beyond-visual-range missile, the Tomahawk cruise missile, the StormBreaker small glide bomb, and the SPY-6 family of advanced naval radars. Demand for these systems surged after Russia's invasion of Ukraine, as NATO allies rushed to replenish stocks and expand inventories.

Raytheon's LTAMDS (Lower Tier Air and Missile Defense Sensor) is slated to replace older Patriot radars, and the segment's Coyote counter-drone system has drawn significant interest as the threat from low-cost unmanned systems proliferates. The segment's challenge has been translating elevated demand into commensurate profitability, as fixed-price development contracts and supply-chain disruptions compressed margins through the early 2020s.

overview.title: Strategic Position and Risks
paragraphs: RTX's integrated span across propulsion, avionics, and weapons gives it rare leverage in the competition for large platform programs, where governments increasingly prefer contractors capable of delivering—and sustaining—complete systems rather than components. Its F-35 engine and Patriot system relationships alone represent decades of recurring revenue.

Key risks include: concentration of U.S. government revenue (roughly 60% of total sales), political exposure around large foreign military sales, the unresolved financial tail of the GTF powder-metal issue, and the long-standing challenge of managing program-level cost overruns on complex fixed-price development contracts. The company has also faced periodic government investigations and contract disputes that are endemic to the sector but generate headline risk.

sourceSuggestions.title: RTX Investor Relations – Annual Reports and Earnings
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Primary source for segment financials, backlog data, and official guidance on GTF remediation costs and program milestones.

sourceSuggestions.title: RTX Corporate Website
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Product and program listings across all three segments; useful for verifying current branding and program status.

sourceSuggestions.title: U.S. Department of Defense Contract Announcements
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Authoritative source for RTX contract awards, values, and scope—essential for tracking revenue visibility and new program wins.

sourceSuggestions.title: Pratt & Whitney GTF Engine Program Updates
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Tracks the F135 program's development and sustainment posture; cross-reference with GTF commercial remediation disclosures to understand propulsion segment health.

sourceSuggestions.title: Congressional Research Service – F-35 Program Reports
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Independent analysis of F-35 cost, schedule, and engine program status; essential context for valuing Pratt & Whitney's most critical military franchise.

sourceSuggestions.title: Collins Aerospace Product Portfolio
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Detailed product catalog covering avionics, interiors, and mission systems; useful for mapping Collins' competitive position against Honeywell, Thales, and others.

sourceSuggestions.title: Raytheon Missiles & Defense Program Overview
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Official program listings for Patriot, AMRAAM, Tomahawk, and SPY-6; cross-reference with DoD contract announcements for demand trajectory.

sourceSuggestions.title: Companies Market Cap – RTX Historical Market Capitalization
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Long-run market cap trend useful for contextualizing RTX's valuation through the merger, GTF crisis, and Ukraine-driven demand surge.
